7.1.label: Question 7.1
7.1.synopsis: Intermediaries need training, increased commission to offset retraining costs, averse to risky product, concerned about mis-selling, need persuading on market size, may prefer more established products
7.1.question: Discuss the considerations that an insurer would need to make in persuading insurance intermediaries to start selling LTCI.

Considerations for persuading intermedia: The insurance intermediaries will need to be suitably trained to sell LTCI.

This training could be provided directly by the insurer. Levels of commission will need to be increased to reflect any additional costs to the sales channels for this retraining – for example, the intermediaries investment in new sales techniques and compensation for time lost whilst being trained.
However, this will only impact on the intermediaries for a short time, and so commission may be reduced after a short period.
The intermediaries may be averse to selling such a risky product.
They may be particularly concerned about the risks of claims of mis-selling or poor advice in the future if the benefits are not clearly understood by the policyholder and their families.
They will therefore need persuading that the market for LTCI, and for the particular product they will be selling, is good enough to enable them to generate worthwhile sales volumes.

This may be difficult in some jurisdictions where demand for LTCI is relatively low. For example, they may be more tempted to start selling a more established health product, such as CI insurance, which is expected to sell better.

7.2.label: Question 7.2
7.2.synopsis: Advantages: more comprehensive, better matches financial need, fair payment, multiple claims, differentiation, comparisons difficult, reduced windfall risk. Disadvantages: complex, reduced windfall appeal, overlaps confuse, lack of data, experience changes rapidly, difficulty defining stages, managing expectations, greater margins/reinsurance needed, earlier claims, complex administration
7.2.question: Outline the advantages and disadvantages of offering severity-based CI cover.

Advantages: The CI insurance product becomes more comprehensive; a benefit is offered at levels of disease progression that would not have triggered payment under a more standard CI insurance contract.
The amount of payments more closely matches financial need, reducing the incentive for anti-selection at outset and exaggeration of symptoms for some claim stages.
The payment amount may also be seen as being fair, and so the product may be popular and easier to sell.
Multiple claims are possible which enhances policyholder satisfaction and retention.
As a variant on the standard product, it permits the insurer to differentiate itself from its competitors.
It makes comparisons more difficult, and so the product may be potentially more profitable.
The potential for windfall payments is reduced, which reduces the risk of losses to the insurer and may allow premiums to be more competitive.
Disadvantages: A more complex product and difficult to compare with other CI insurance products; this may reduce its appeal to the prospective policyholder or to brokers.
The appeal to customers of potentially obtaining a windfall payment is also reduced.
Overlaps between related illnesses may confuse customers and increase the potential for claim disputes. It will also make pricing more complex.
Lack of reliable data in the level of detail required.
Experience may change too rapidly in future for data to build up with sufficient credibility.
Difficulties in defining each stage of the disease to trigger each level of benefit. Definitions need to be both legally and medically objective, and understandable to the consumer.
Policyholders may be disappointed if they do not get the full sum insured on claim, and so their expectations need to be managed.
Greater need for margins, less potential for guarantees, and greater need for reinsurance (eg for assistance).
Claims will occur earlier in the policy term, which increases the importance of pre-existing conditions and so increases the seriousness of any material misrepresentation.
Claims administration and claims underwriting will be more complex and expensive.

7.3.label: Question 7.3
7.3.synopsis: Genetic tests enable individuals to know predispositions and take more care; may lead to increased health insurance applications (adverse selection if regulation restricts insurer use), earlier CI claims due to accelerated diagnosis, fewer CI claims if disease prevented, fewer/later PMI claims if care taken, fewer/shorter IP claims if policyholder stays working longer; all need incorporating into premium rates
7.3.question: Discuss how the increasing availability and popularity of genetic testing could affect the pricing assumptions for health and care insurance products?

Impact of genetic testing on pricing ass: It is possible for persons who have had genetic tests:
• to know if they have increased pre-dispositions to certain kinds of illness, particularly cancer
• to take more care in both looking after their health and screening themselves for certain illnesses.

• increased applications for health insurance from such individuals, who may not be required to divulge all the information to the insurer if the use of genetic information was restricted by regulation – this could lead to worse claims experience
• earlier claims (CI insurance) due to accelerated diagnosis
• fewer claims if earlier screening prevents the disease (CI insurance)
• fewer / later claims if increased care taken by the policyholder averts or delays treatments (PMI)
• fewer / shorter claims if increased care taken by the policyholder allows them to keep working for longer / recover quicker. (IP)
The impact of each of these would need to be incorporated into the premium rates, if possible, whilst maintaining a product that is competitive and meets customers' needs.

7.4.label: Question 7.4
7.4.scenario:
A health and care insurer is considering a proposal to enter into contracts with organisations providing long-term care in nursing homes for the elderly.

The nursing homes will be divided into three categories:
• those providing nursing care for the frail elderly, but who do not provide 24-hour care
• those providing full 24-hour nursing care
• those providing full 24-hour nursing care for those with cognitive impairments.

It plans to use these contracts to provide immediate needs long-term care insurance policies. These policies will be available to those who have been admitted to any care home with which the insurer has a contract for the provision of care.

The immediate relatives of these people will also be able to buy a policy, secured on the life of the person needing care.

In return for a single payment at the time of admission, the policy will guarantee to meet the cost of care in the nursing home to which the insured is admitted until they die. The policies will not acquire a surrender value at any time.

(i).synopsis: Policy design: security except for deterioration risk, indemnity basis exposes to care fee changes, counterparty risk, contractual relationships, reputation risk. Underwriting: medical underwriting, 30-day admission rule, different procedures, no financial underwriting. Pricing: longevity risk, limited data, national data adjusted, low maintenance expenses, high initial sale expenses, capital intensive, purchase price factors, contingency loadings, cross-subsidies, investment strategy. Selling: complex product, restrict to trained advisers, cover on life of another widens market. Administration: systems for care provider payments
(i).question: Describe the factors that the insurer should consider in designing, underwriting, pricing and selling this policy. You should also consider the effects on the contracts with the long-term care organisations.

Policy design: The policy provides security for the insured... [½]
... except for the risk that their health deteriorates so that the care that can be provided by the home, to which they were originally admitted, is no longer able to care for them. [½]
Assuming that the original benefit will continue in payment, the increased costs of providing care in a more appropriate home will need to be paid by the insured, ... [½]
... either directly or through the purchase of another immediate needs policy. [½]
Alternatively the insured may end up with inappropriate care. [½]
The policy is new, and so policyholders will have no other policies with which it can be compared. [½]
As a result of this lack of competition, it will probably be sufficient for the insurer to make sure that the policy provides good care, rather than the very best quality care. [½]
The policy is providing indemnity rather than cash benefits, and so the insurer in exposed to the risk of any change in nursing care fees. [½]
In part this is controlled by the contractual relationship with the organisations managing the homes. [½]
If the insured can arrange contracts with prices fixed for the expected duration of care in a home of a given type, then part of this risk will be controlled. [½]
The insurer is exposed to counterparty risks (eg the risk that an organisation running the homes becomes insolvent). [½]

Not only will the insurer want competitive contracts with the organisations, it will also want to ensure that the organisations provide good quality care and are run on sound financial principles. [½]
Care costs are likely to inflate at a higher rate than general price inflation, because the largest component of the organisations' costs will be salaries for medically trained staff. [½]
The insurer should therefore build realistic wage inflation into the negotiated contracts with the organisations running the homes. [½]
It should be a condition of the policy that any statutory care benefits received by the insured are offset against the benefits provided by the policy. [½]
The insured will perceive that the insurer, rather than the organisation, is the provider of care, so any inadequacies in care may be blamed on the insurer. [½]
So the insurer is exposed to the risk of losing its reputation or being involved in protracted disputes about the care provided. [½]
Underwriting: These policies would be subject to medical underwriting in order to establish the degree of impairment of the insured and thus the single premium that should be charged. [½]
To avoid selection against the office, the insurer should require that a policy is taken out within a short period, say 30 days, of the person being admitted to the nursing home. [½]
Policies could be issued after this 30-day deadline provided they were subject to detailed underwriting. [½]
This is to prevent those in better health (and thus expecting to receive more support than the average life) selecting against the office. [½]
The underwriting procedures for these policies will be different from those used for the insurer's current product range. [½]
There must be sufficient trained staff to cope with the new underwriting procedures. [½]
There will be no need for financial underwriting because the indemnity benefit is being paid directly to the providers. [½]
Pricing: The insurer is exposed to the risk of lives living longer than expected. [½]

This may result from the mortality of nursing home residents improving, perhaps as the result of advances in medical technology. Any mortality rates reflecting current experience should allow for future improvements. [½]
The policy is new, and so there will be limited data on which to determine prices. [½]

It may be possible to use national data on the mortality of those in nursing homes and to adjust them for the selective effects of the policy and consequently with lighter mortality than the national rates. [½]
For example, the policy will tend to be bought by those with greater wealth and by those who feel they have a relatively good life expectancy. [½]
The standard of care provided by the homes (assuming it is of good quality) may further improve mortality rates. [½]
The policy-maintenance expenses will be low as: [¼]
• there are no renewal premiums to collect [¼]
• there are a limited number of homes with which the insurer has contracts that will submit bills for care costs [¼]
• the insurer does not have to monitor the insured's health. [¼]
Expenses will be limited to queries, terminations, etc. [½]
The major part of the expenses will be the expenses of the initial sale. The expenses will need to include the costs of any advice the insurer provides. [½]
This may be more involved than for other health and care policies because the insured will be frail and explanations and arrangements may be protracted. [½]
However, marketing costs will be low because potential purchasers can be identified at little or no cost at their time of admission to a care home. [½]

Despite the fact that the product has a single premium, it may be capital intensive because supervisory reserves, solvency capital and the initial expenses are likely to exceed the single premium. [1]
This initial capital strain may be mitigated by suitable reinsurance contracts. [½]

The purchase price of the care benefits would depend on the type of nursing home to which the insured has been admitted, age, gender (if permitted by legislation) and the type and extent of the insured's impairment. [1]
The insurer needs to determine the size of the contingency loadings needed in the premiums. [½]

The contractual arrangement with the care home organisations reduces the impact of the guaranteed benefits, but the insurer will need to consider how sensitive profit levels are to the key assumptions being made. [½]
The insurer should consider the extent to which cross-subsidies are appropriate (eg younger policyholders subsidising older policyholders, females subsidising males). [½]
Cross-subsidies may enable the insurer to use a simpler rating structure or to attract more business in total. [½]

The policies are of a relatively short term so investing funds in fixed-interest and index-linked government and corporate bonds would be appropriate. The expected return should be reflected in the pricing basis. [½]
Selling: The product has a complex structure, and so the insurer may wish to restrict sales to approved financial advisers or tied agents to whom it has provided training and advice. [½]
This would help prevent mis-selling and possible damage to the reputation of the product and the insurer. [½]
Explicitly offering cover on the 'life of another' may widen the market and offer the possibility of increased sales. [½]
Administration: Administrative systems should be capable of making payments to care providers, dealing with enquiries from policyholders, for example. [¼]

(ii).synopsis: (a) Option: prevents selection, closer match to needs, reduces reputation risk, but substantial additional costs if transfer to 24-hour care, additional uncertainty in pricing, multiple-state model needed, data scarce. (b) Simple underwriting: popular due to urgency, homogeneous mortality via admission criteria, age key factor, gender cannot be used, location effect, medical reports as alternative, legislative risk. (c) Capital guarantee: reduces longevity risk, sum at risk falls over time, reduces contingency margin, premiums less sensitive to mortality, increased perceived value and sales potential, cross-subsidy benefit, investment matching conflict, policyholder communication needed
(ii).question:
The marketing department has produced the following suggestions for improving the product:

(a) The policy should have an option to cover the additional cost of care, if the insured's health deteriorates to such an extent that they need to transfer to a nursing home in another category. The option can only be included in the policy at the time the original policy is purchased.

(b) Underwriting should be restricted to a simple proposal form that asks for basic demographic information (ie age and location only).

(c) The immediate needs insurance policy should have a capital guarantee, such that on the death of the insured, the purchase price of the policy less any nursing home fees paid would be paid as a death benefit.

Comment on the suitability of each of these suggestions.

(a) Option to cover additional costs of: The option will need to be arranged so as to prevent selection against the insurer. [½]
For example, those in failing health, or their relatives, buying the option knowing that they expect to need more expensive care shortly. [½]
The option will ensure that the policy is a closer match to the insured's needs because it provides for all future care costs. [½]
It significantly reduces the risk that the insurer is accused of not 'providing the care that the policyholder thought was promised', with the consequent threat to the insurer's reputation. [½]

Only the costs of care additional to those incurred in the previous home are covered. However, these could be substantial if the change is to a home that provided 24-hour care or care for those with a cognitive impairment. [½]
The option will introduce additional uncertainty into the estimation of the expected future claim costs. The option premium will need to include a substantial contingency loading. [½]
So the option could need a large additional premium. [½]
We will need to model the transfers in a multiple-state model. This will allow us to estimate the probabilities of transfer in the longer term. [½]
The transfer rates may be influenced by management policy in the homes rather than solely by medical need. [½]
Our close relationship with the homes may enable us to control this risk. [½]
We could also control some of this risk by suitable reinsurance. [½]
In the short term there may be no suitable data from which the rates can be estimated reliably. [½]
This difficulty will be mitigated somewhat by the fact that this is a rider to the main policy, and so there can be an element of cross subsidy between the main policy and the rider. [½]
There may be additional policy maintenance and claim management expenses as a result of each transfer. [½]
(b) Simple underwriting: It will be important that proposals can be processed quickly and a decision on acceptance can be made quickly. [½]
The insured is frail and needs care, delays will not be welcome. So a simple underwriting procedure would be popular. [½]
We need to ensure that the mortality experience of those purchasing policies is fairly homogeneous. [½]

If the nursing home has strict criteria for admission, only taking those for whom care is essential, then it is likely that age will be the rating factor having the most significant effect on future mortality. [½]
Gender is also likely to have a significant impact on future mortality but as this is not able to be used as a rating factor an average mix of business will need to be assumed. [1]
Location will have an effect on expected future mortality, along with the expected cost of cover. [½]
The simple proposal form will therefore be satisfactory provided we are happy with the admission policies of the homes. [½]
Alternatively we could ask the insured for permission to see the medical reports that led to the recommendation for admission. [½]
The types of care provided by the different homes should ensure that with any type of home the mortality experience of those admitted will be relatively homogeneous. [½]
If we could not rely on the selective effect of the admission criteria, we would need to price on the basis of the lightest expected mortality within each group. [½]
This may lead to higher premium rates and result in reduced business volumes. [½]
If the policy is indeed unique to the insurer then the possibilities of selection against the office are reduced because 'good' risks do not have an alternative provider. [½]
However, 'bad risks' (ie those with lighter mortality) are still more likely to buy the policy, so a selection effect is still present. [½]
The restricted risk classification (ie only age and location) may increase the extent of cross-subsidies within each risk group. [½]
Changes to legislation affecting the underwriting process (eg age being prohibited from use as a rating factor) may lead to a review of the underwriting policy or contract design in the future. [½]
Any likely future legislative restrictions should be allowed for now to avoid a costly re-launch of the product. [½]
(c) Policy with a capital guarantee: The sum at risk on the capital guarantee decreases as the term of the policy increases. [½]
However, the lives insured are very old (so the likelihood of death is high), and so the expected additional claims costs and resulting increase in premium could be substantial. [½]
The longevity risk will be reduced due to the existence of the death benefit. [½]

If mortality is lighter than expected, the loss the insurer makes due to paying benefits for longer is partially offset by the fact it will pay a lower death benefit, if any (and later than expected). [½]
This will reduce the contingency margin in the premiums and in the reserves needed. Hence the initial capital strain will be reduced (because reserves will be lower). [½]
Premiums will be less sensitive to the mortality assumptions, and so there may be less need for detailed underwriting. [½]
The addition of the option may increase the perceived value of the policy to some people as early death has no financial downside for the policyholder. [½]
This may result in increased sales despite the increased premium required. This relies in part on the insurer remaining the sole provider of the product. [½]

The option will allow some cross-subsidy. For example, falling mortality will increase the expected costs of the main benefits but decrease the expected costs of the capital guarantee. This will mean that the importance of estimating future mortality rates accurately is reduced. [1]

Choosing investments to match the liabilities will be more difficult because there is a conflict between the need to match the longer term liabilities for care costs and the shorter term liabilities of the capital guarantee. [½]
Will need to keep the policyholder regularly informed of the care costs incurred to avoid disputes about the amount payable on death. [½]

(iii).synopsis: General: both novel and differentiate product, survey suggests policyholder appeal, potential savings on fixed costs if sales increase, need to investigate whether savings fund benefit. Holiday: not universally desired, substantial additional care costs (transport, short-term stay rates), administrative difficulty, annual recurring cost, health improvement risk, frail elderly may not enjoy it in reality. Funeral: one-off cost for all, few weeks benefit cost, universal perceived value, less direct enjoyment than holiday, needs cash limit or named directors, control costs via contracts, exclude special arrangements, or inflation-linked cash limit
(iii).question:
These immediate needs policies (as described in part (i) and without any of the marketing department's suggested improvements) have been sold for the last two years. Sales have matched the original business plan, largely because no other company has offered a similar product. Attracted by the insurer's success, two other insurers are planning to launch a similar product.

With the aim of combating the expected competition, the marketing department has conducted a survey of existing policyholders to find out which additional benefits they would find most attractive.

The two benefits suggested most frequently are:

(a) The policy should provide additional nursing fees for two weeks each year to allow the insured a holiday in a nursing home by the sea.

(b) The policy should provide an indemnity to pay the cost of the insured's funeral.

Compare these two benefits in the context of the suggestion that they should be added to current and new policies without any change in premium rates.

General issues: Both benefits are novel, and so they may help to distinguish the policy from those of its competitors. [½]
The survey results suggest that a large number of existing policyholders like the ideas, and so they may appeal to a similar proportion of new policyholders. [½]
If the features increase sales, they may generate savings on fixed costs that can help to fund the benefits. [½]
Investigations should be made to see if the savings in each case are sufficient to fund the benefit. [½]
(a) Holiday benefit: Not everybody will want a holiday by the sea (particularly those already in a home by the sea!), and so the benefit will have no perceived value for some policyholders. [½]
In fact they may feel that they are paying for something they will not make use of. [½]

The expected costs are the additional costs of care in a home by the sea. These may be substantial because it is unlikely that the usual nursing home will be able save anything other than incidental 'hotel' costs. [½]
Short-term residence by the sea may be more expensive per week than long-term residence. [½]
Transportation costs may be substantial, particularly for the frail elderly. [½]
Costs may be controlled if the insurer can enter into contracts with the homes by the sea, particularly if the contracts can offer guaranteed prices. [½]
Even so, the administrative difficulties and costs may be substantial. [½]
The benefit is an annual one, and so costs will be greater than the one-time funeral benefit. The costs are likely to increase each year as the result of inflation. [½]
A holiday by the sea may improve the insured's health and so might increase the insurer's expected costs. [½]

The survey respondents may like the idea, but not the reality (eg disrupting the routine of the frail elderly), so the extension may not be a success if it is implemented. Those who buy the option may in retrospect think it is poor value, even though it is 'free'. [½]
(b) Funeral benefit: This is a one-off cost at the end of the contract for all policyholders. [½]
It will probably have an expected cost equal to a few weeks of benefit. [½]
It will have a perceived value for all policyholders. [½]
It may not be such an attractive feature as the holiday benefit. It gives peace of mind, but no direct enjoyment. [½]
The insurer will need to specify exactly what benefits are offered, either in terms of a cash limit or in terms of the funeral directors who can be employed. [½]
The insurer could control costs by entering into contracts with firms of funeral directors. [½]
The insurer may want to exclude special arrangements (eg burial at sea, scattering ashes on mountain tops) as these will increase costs substantially. [½]
Alternatively the policy could include an inflation-linked cash limit on this benefit. [½]

7.5.label: Question 7.5
7.5.scenario:
An established short-term health insurance company in a developed country writes private medical insurance and short-term accident and sickness insurance only. Its products are distributed through specialist brokers, and it also has an arrangement with a major bank to sell its products. In the case of the latter, most business is sold to customers taking out a loan, such as a mortgage.

The country has a comprehensive State healthcare system available free to all citizens.

It is considering introducing postcode as a new rating factor on all of its products. Postcodes are used to identify geographical location fairly precisely – for example, a full postcode will usually be unique to a group of 15 or fewer properties within an area.

(i).synopsis: Claims experience: health varies by location due to pollution, stress, proximity to others, climate, social network, diet, smoking/alcohol, lifestyle, proximity to dangers, access to facilities, housing standard, education level; income/wealth differences; occupation; economic conditions, attitudes to claiming, cost of private treatment, quality of State healthcare. New business: income levels, political allegiances, bank customer propensity by area, loan proportions and sizes, broker customer types by area. Renewal rates: affordability of premium increases, loan default rates by location
(i).question: Outline the reasons why geographical location might affect the experience on the insurer's products and give examples to illustrate these effects.

Impact on claims experience: The general health of a customer will influence their expected claim experience. [½]
The health of the population may vary by location due to many factors, such as differences in:
• pollution levels, which will be worse in large industrial towns and cities
• exposure to stress, which is generally believed to be less in rural areas
• proximity to other people – eg if you live in an area where public transport is used a lot, you may be more likely to catch a contagious illness
• climate – eg people living in cold, damp regions may be less healthy than those in sunnier parts
• social or family network – eg people living in close-knit communities may be happier and better looked after
• diet – cultural (or climate) differences between regions may be reflected in the quality of food people eat (eg prevalence of unhealthy food in one area)
• the incidence of smoking and alcohol consumption, which may vary across the country and could have a big impact on sickness claims
• lifestyle – eg people living in the country or by the sea may be more active and exposed to the outdoors than those in cities
• proximity to dangers – eg people living close to a busy road may be more likely to be involved in a traffic accident
• access to facilities that can improve health, such as good food or health clubs – eg a popular health-food restaurant chain may have opened in a particular region
• standard of housing – eg some areas may have people living in cramped or unhygienic conditions
• level of education – eg those living in areas with better schooling (or access to healthcare) may be more health conscious and know better how to look after themselves.
[½ each for idea and example]
Differences in the levels of income or wealth will contribute to many of the above factors. [½]
For example, wealthier people can better afford things that will improve their health, such as good food and preventative healthcare ... [½]
... although, arguably, the well-off may better afford things that can harm their body, such as rich food or recreational drugs. [½]
Occupation varies by geographical location, and occupation is correlated with health. [½]
Other reasons why claim experience will vary by location include differences in:
• economic conditions – eg those living in areas of high unemployment may be less happy in their jobs and so more tempted to claim on their policies [½]
• attitudes to claiming – eg in some areas it may be more socially acceptable to be absent from work or to make fraudulent claims [½]
• the cost of private treatment, which will affect PMI claim costs, ... [½]
• eg this may be more expensive in some areas where salaries are generally higher, or there might be preferred providers (of cheaper treatment) in certain areas but not in others [½]

• quality and access to healthcare services – the standard of State healthcare may vary by location, and so those people that can be treated better or sooner by the State may be less likely to claim on PMI. [½]
• Also, they are more likely to have better health generally, and so be less likely to claim on either type of policy. [½]

(The impact on the levels of new business volumes and persistency is also relevant here, because these might affect the premium that should be charged. In addition, if the propensity to buy in certain areas is low, the insurer may decide to reduce premiums to attract this business.)
Impact on new business levels: Many of the factors mentioned above will also affect the likelihood of a customer to purchase one of the insurer's products, and so new business volume levels will vary by location. [½]
Economic differences, such as in levels of income, will be the main influence here. For example, many people in poor areas cannot afford PMI. [½]
Similarly, political allegiances vary by location, and these will influence the extent to which people want PMI. [½]

The propensity of people who are bank customers will vary by area. Indeed, (for historic or competitive reasons) the bank may have a greater prevalence in different parts of the country compared to others. [½]
In addition, the proportions of people requiring loans or mortgages will vary by area. [½]
Also, the size of those loans, and hence the size of accident and sickness insurance policy purchased will vary. [½]

Similarly, customers who tend to use specialist brokers (eg fairly wealthy and/or financially aware types) are more likely to live in certain areas, such as where there are better-paid jobs, and so there will be more of these brokers in certain areas. [½]
Impact on renewal rates: The ability of customers to be able to afford to renew policies in the face of premium rate increases will vary by location (eg depending on salary levels). [½]
Customers in some locations may be more likely to give up, or default on, loans compared to others. This will result in accident and sickness policies not renewing. [½]
Changes in many of the factors mentioned above may also affect renewal rates in certain areas. [½]

(ii).synopsis: Advantages: premiums reflect risk more closely, less anti-selection, better experience and profitability, competitive necessity where brokers use postcode, proxy for other rating factors, fewer application questions, greater profit certainty and lower capital, better reinsurance terms, easy to obtain/verify, familiar from motor. Disadvantages: difficult to justify to customers who move, seen as unfair between adjacent areas, systems need adaptation, difficult to implement at full postcode level, over-complicates rating structure, rate cards cannot be used, sellers need training, some customers face large premium increases, insufficient data at full postcode level, grouping weakens advantages
(ii).question: Explain the advantages and disadvantages of introducing postcode as a rating factor.

Advantages: It will allow the insurer to be able to charge a premium that reflects the expected risk more closely. [½]
It will therefore be less exposed to anti-selection, in that a greater proportion of the better risks and fewer of the higher risks will now take out (or renew) policies. [½]
This should lead to better experience overall, and hence cheaper premiums generally or higher profitability for the insurer. [½]

This is more important in a competitive situation, such as where business is being sold through brokers, if competing insurers are using postcode as a rating factor. Otherwise, the insurer may be left with only the poorer risks. [½]
The use of postcode can be a proxy to other rating factors that the insurer may wish to use to reflect the risk better – for example, lifestyle factors such as gym membership. [½]
It therefore may reduce the need to ask certain other questions on application, and thus allow an easier sell. [½]
Rating more accurately will increase the certainty of future profits, and so this may allow the insurer to reduce its capital requirements. [½]
Reinsurers may also offer better reinsurance terms. [½]
Postcode is an easy measure to obtain and to verify. [½]
It may be easy to justify the use of postcode as a rating factor to some customers, as they may be familiar with this in other insurance products, such as motor. [½]
Disadvantages: It may be difficult to explain or justify to customers – for example, why their premium has changed just because they have moved house. [½]
Similarly, it could be seen to be unfair – for example, people in different but adjacent postcode areas may be paying different premiums. [½]
Systems, such as pricing and administration, will need to be adapted to allow for the new rating factor. [½]

It may be difficult to implement a rating structure, particularly if a large number of postcodes are used (ie down to full postcode level). It therefore may not be worth doing, in relation to the benefits obtained. [½]
The introduction of postcode may over-complicate what is likely to be a fairly simple rating structure, given the products and distribution channels involved. [½]
Simple 'rate cards' could no longer be used, and automated quotation systems may be required instead. These may be unpopular (with sellers and customers alike). [½]
The change will need to be justified to sellers, who may also need further training or financial incentives (commission) to deal with the new rating structure. [½]
Some customers may experience significant increases in their premium rates as a result of the change, which may cause complaints, poor publicity and high levels of non-renewal. [½]
Brokers and the bank may also be unhappy with this. [½]
Alternatively, some smoothing of premium rates from year to year for each customer (or postcode) would be required, but this will require checking (and hence system development). [½]
There is unlikely to be sufficient experience data to be able to price the product at this level of detail (even if the insurer covered all, say, 15 households in a full postcode area). [½]
Hence, much grouping over postcodes will be required, which starts to weaken the advantages outlined above. [½]

Even after significant grouping, the demand for these products from certain locations may be so low, that its effect on the required premium could not be ascertained (eg few people will buy PMI in relatively poor areas). [½]
[Maximum 9]
